title: Foucault, Derrida, Deleuze: How Three Thinkers Became a Convenient Trinity

source.original: Today I Deconstruct Deconstruction — Brivael Le Pogam

body_html:
In the populist critique of postmodernism, three names recur like an incantation: Foucault, Derrida, Deleuze. Brivael Le Pogam's thread crystallizes this tendency by naming them as the co-architects of deconstruction — a unified project supposedly manufactured between 1966 and 1980. But collapsing these three radically different thinkers into a single conspiratorial bloc requires significant intellectual shortcuts.

Foucault's work centered on power, institutions, and the archaeology of knowledge. Derrida developed deconstruction as a method of reading texts against their own assumptions. Deleuze pursued a philosophy of difference, desire, and immanence that often ran counter to Derrida's project. The three had notable disagreements — Deleuze and Derrida occupied different philosophical camps, and Foucault's relationship with Derrida was famously contentious after their clash over Descartes and madness.

Yet in the reactionary imaginary, these distinctions dissolve. What matters is the narrative utility: three French intellectuals, a specific time window, and a target — Western civilization. The trinity format provides a clean story with identifiable antagonists, which is far more shareable than a nuanced account of post-structuralism's internal debates.

This flattening is itself a kind of intellectual operation — one that mirrors, ironically, the very reductionism that serious engagement with these thinkers would caution against. The signal here is not about whether these philosophers were right or wrong but about how their legacies are being weaponized in contemporary culture wars.
